blocks.content: From the next day onward, Shirone began working at the library.
He realized why this job had to be done alone.
The document Temuran handed him listed hundreds of titles, and the categorization criteria were subjective.
For example, should *The History of Swordsmanship* be classified as history or swordsmanship?
Though Temuran had provided a basic system, dividing ten thousand books required many arbitrary judgments.
Shirone decided to prioritize. History was broad, while swordsmanship was specialized, so *The History of Swordsmanship* was categorized under swordsmanship.
Just establishing these standards took two weeks.
But even this task was beneficial to Shirone.
While creating the categorization system, he realized that all knowledge is interconnected.
Music and mathematics, chemistry and the mind, art and science—they weren't so different.
Once the system was in place, the work gained momentum.
As he began meeting the targets on time, Shirone finally turned his attention to studying.
Knowledge was the greatest divider between nobles and commoners.
The knowledge he'd gain here would be immensely helpful in the future.
Shirone decided to use his time efficiently.
With ten thousand books before him, even two years wouldn't be enough to read them all.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: *Where should I start? Should I just read whatever comes my way?*
A good idea struck Shirone, and he slapped his knee.
Since all knowledge is connected, mastering one field would make it easier to supplement others later.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Then which field should I focus on?
After much thought, Shirone's lips curled into a smile.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Study history.
If human life is history, then knowledge has also evolved alongside it.
Learning history first was like building the spine of a creature.
In his mind, Shirone drew a horizon of history.
Zooming in, that line contained all human knowledge—religion, science, magic, mythology, literature, art, politics, war, economics—arranged chronologically.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Mastering history will allow me to supplement other knowledge anytime.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, it'll make combining them much faster.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: History is the spine of knowledge.
The grand library held roughly 850 history books.
Shirone made a decision.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: *In two years, I'll read all 850 of these books. I'll build the spine of knowledge!*
From that day on, Shirone stayed in the library to read even after work ended.
Though he skipped roll calls under the pretext of working overtime, no one except Temuran cared about a temporary worker like Shirone.
Reading history books was honestly grueling.
Having lived in the mountains for 16 years, he had no context for the continent's unfamiliar place names, people, or the events tied to them.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: *Memorization records in the mind, but understanding records in the heart. Even a single line of events must be fully understood to build the spine of knowledge.*
Yet, understanding history was backbreaking labor.
It wasn't just about events—he had to meticulously analyze why these events were inevitable and how they influenced future ones.
His efficiency dropped drastically, and at one point, he couldn't finish a single book in a week.
He often slept in the library, collapsing unconscious more often than not.
4 AM.
The iron door opened, and Temuran entered, holding a crystal lamp.
Shirone didn't notice, but Temuran had been coming at this hour every day to monitor him.
Temuran picked up the book Shirone had dropped while reading.
Despite the content not being particularly difficult, the book hadn't changed in half a month.
Temuran scoffed.

blocks.speaker: Temuran
blocks.content: *If he'd been frantically studying practical books, I'd have considered banning him from entering outside work hours.*

blocks.speaker: Temuran
blocks.content: *But now, it's clear he's completely clueless. Just because he can read a little, he's dreaming absurd dreams. What a fool.*

blocks.speaker: Temuran
blocks.content: As expected. What success could a lowborn possibly achieve?
With a lighter heart, Temuran turned away.
To rise through studying, you need luck, but more importantly, brains.
Shirone, with his grand dreams, was already doomed.
Three months had passed since Shirone began working at the library.
As he grew accustomed to the categorization work, his time management became more relaxed, and he learned many things from the servants.
The most shocking revelation was that the nobles didn't care at all whether the books were leaked or not.
After all, the family's secret techniques were only passed down directly, and nobles had so many responsibilities that they couldn't care less about a few missing books.
That didn't make Shirone any less at risk.
The nobles' leniency was built on the loyalty of rigid commoners.
The servants could be ruthless to prevent even the slightest mistake.
Shirone felt firsthand how vastly different the mindsets of the two classes were.
What was trivial to nobles could be a life-or-death matter for commoners.
At noon, the library door suddenly burst open, and a boy's voice shattered the silence.

blocks.speaker: Ozent Rian
blocks.content: Ugh, damn old man. What's wrong with taking a break on a day like this?
Shirone blinked at the uninvited guest.
It was the family head's youngest son, Ozent Rian.
True to his martial lineage, he had a robust southern face and stood a head taller than Shirone, despite being the same age.
A loud shout came from outside the library.

blocks.speaker: Ozent Rian
blocks.content: Rian! How dare you run away? If I catch you, it's an extra hundred laps in the garden!
Yikes! Damn it!
Rian fidgeted, darting between bookshelves before spotting Shirone.
Without giving him time to react, Rian shook him and yelled.

blocks.speaker: Ozent Rian
blocks.content: Hey! Don't tell anyone I'm here. Got it?
Before Shirone could reply, Rian hid under a bookshelf.
Simultaneously, the door opened, and an old man's roar shook the library.

blocks.speaker: Old Man
blocks.content: Riaan! I know you're hiding here!
Though white-haired, the old man had broad shoulders and stood as tall as a giant.
His white beard reached his chest as he glared at Shirone with menacing eyes.

blocks.speaker: Old Man
blocks.content: You! Did a green-haired peanut-sized brat come in here?
Shirone wondered if he knew such a person.
He knew a blue-haired boy, but if that was considered peanut-sized, how did the old man perceive him?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Judging by the description, it sounds like the young master.
Excuse me, but who are you?
Shirone planned to buy time by invoking the family's authority.
But the old veteran, who had commanded battlefields for decades, saw right through him.

blocks.speaker: Old Man
blocks.content: Swordsmanship instructor! The only one who can beat that peanut into shape! Stop wasting time and answer me.
Unless you want a beating yourself.
Shirone swallowed his words.
To those who sought knowledge, a teacher was as revered as the heavens.
Especially one entrusted by the family head—no servant's wordplay would work on him.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Ah, I see. The young master is hiding under that bookshelf.
Hey! Are you crazy? Want to die?
Rian crawled out, scowling.
But when he saw the swordsmanship instructor right in front of him, he trembled like a leaf.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: Master, this is a misunderstanding!
You little—!
Grabbed by the collar, Rian was slammed to the ground.
As Rian's body flew in an arc, Shirone winced.
Rian sprawled on the floor, limbs twitching.
Follow me, you brat. Forget training—you need to rebuild your mental discipline first.
Dragged away by his leg, Rian craned his neck to look at Shirone.
He mouthed something silently.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: *You're dead.*
